text: The sampler-enricher for the Molten-Salt Reactor experiment was designed to remove 10-g samples and to add 90-g increments of uranium to the pump bowl.
During the five-year service life the equipment was used to isolate 593 samples and to make 152 fuel additions.
The boots were changed 17 times.

The experiment served as a test of (1) the compatibility of the fluoride salt with the structural material and the graphite moderator and (2) the stability of the salt during extended operation in a high-radiation field.

Two streams of fuel salt, having a combined flow rate of about 65 gpm, flowed from the circulating loop through this region.<sup>1</sup> Salt volume in this reservoir, which served as the expansion volume for the fuel loop, was about 30 gallons.
Two barriers were required between any source of radioactivity greater than 1000 curies and the atmosphere.
Anticipated operating life was one year or about 1,000 sampling cycles.
The design pressure was 50 psig for primary containment areas and 40 psig for secondary containment areas.
Design temperature was $100^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
Figure 1 is a schematic diagram of the sampler-enricher.
1) to the gas space of the pump bowl.
The transfer cask was placed in a sealed container bolted to the frame of a truck and was moved to the high-radiation level analytical chemistry facility located in the X-10 area of ORNL.

The barriers were (1) the operational valve and the maintenance valve which separated 1C from the pump bowl (1A); (2) the access port which separated 1C from 3A; and (3) the removal valve which provided access to 3A from the operating area.
Ten-Gram Sample Capsule - About 10 grams of salt was required for routine chemical analysis.
The capsule was $3/4$ in. in diameter by 1.6 in. long with hemispherical top and bottom.

A key was attached to the top of the capsule by a loop of 1/32-in.-diam stranded-steel rope, which provided the flexibility necessary for the sample assembly to pass through the two 15-in.-radius bends in the transfer tube.
Before use copper capsules were hydrogen fired at $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ for two hours to remove surface contaminants and the oxide film, since oxide interfered with the uranium analysis.
Enriching Capsules - Figure 2 also shows an enriching capsule assembly.
The capsule was fabricated from 3/4-in. by 0.035-in.-wall nickel tubing.
The bottom was spun shut on a 3/8-in. radius.
The capsule was 6-3/8 in. long and contained about $120\mathrm{g}$ of enriching salt (90 g of ${}^{235}\mathrm{U}$ ).

A complete description of and specifications for the instrumentation for the sampler-enricher is given in the MSRE Design and Operations Report.4,5 A brief discussion is given below for a few major components.
The pressure drop was about 0.8 psi per cc/min of gas flow.
When a barrier was closed and sealed, the pressure was essentially the same as the buffer gas supply or 40 psig.
The drive unit cable was driven by a gear so that one revolution of the drive unit shaft produced a positive 8-in. movement of the cable.

After the pressure of 1C was adjusted with helium to match that of the pump bowl, the operational and maintenance valves were opened and the capsule was lowered 17 ft 5 in. to reach the pump bowl level.
It was then withdrawn 18 in. into a vertical section of the transfer tube and held 10 minutes to allow the salt to solidify to prevent spilling while moving through the sloping section of the tube.
The area was purged with helium for 30 minutes, then evacuated and refilled with clean helium.
For the initial fueling of the reactor with $^{235}\mathrm{U}$ the capsules were stored in a vault at the reactor site until they were needed.
Empty capsules were sent to the high radiation level laboratory for final weighing since the activity level was much too high $(>1,000\mathrm{R/hr}$ at 3 in.) to allow handling in the glove box.
When $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ and Pu were added to the reactor, the same type of capsules were used.
The $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ capsules had to be handled in a shielded facility, cell G of the Thorium Uranium
Recycle Facility, because of the 222 ppm of $^{232}\mathrm{U}$ in the feed and its associated radiation.
The radiation level was about 1 R/hr at 3 inches for these capsules.
During the five-year period that the reactor was operated, 593 sampling cycles and 152 fuel enrichments were made.
While the capsule was being attached to the latch, the operational valve was closed so a dropped capsule fell only about 15 inches below the access port opening to the gate of the valve.

Based on feel and sound the second capsule could be lifted a few inches in the pump bowl but was not recovered.<sup>9</sup> It is believed to have moved between the mist shield and the guide cage, preventing retrieval.
Later this latch was replaced with another one of similar dimensions, which was fabricated of 430 stainless steel instead of 304 stainless steel so that it was magnetic.
One of the bevel gears connecting the cable drive unit to its motor started slipping on its shaft in December 1968 after 15 months of use.
Next, a 3-in.-diam hole was drilled through the 1/2-in.-thick wall of the drive unit box at a point adjacent to the gears.
At the start of this repair operation the radiation level at the gears was $45\mathrm{R / hr}$ .
At the outside of the containment box where the hole was drilled it was $27\mathrm{R / hr}$ .
As a result of this, the upper and lower limit switches and the 4-in. permissive switch for the operational and maintenance valves were bypassed for the remaining life of the drive unit.
The inch hand reading varied about $1/2$ in. from insertion to withdrawal direction.
The foot indicator varied $3/4$ ft so that the operator had to mentally compensate for the differences to be certain of the exact location of the capsule.
The position indicator distance readings were reproducible to $< 1/4$ in.
The operational valve had sheared the cable when it was closed.9 Calculations made later indicated that an 8-lb force on the hand wheel of the valve was sufficient to shear the cable.
The sudden stop snapped the wire rope holding the capsule allowing it to fall to the bottom of the pump bowl.10,11 The latch was retrieved during a four-week maintenance effort.
Figure 6 shows the recovery tooling and containment.
Figure 7 shows the latch and key after removal from transfer line.
The latch fell on its side allowing the key to slip out of it and the capsule to drop into the pump bowl.[12] The capsule recovery attempt with magnets was described in a prior section.
8, was mounted on the transfer tube between the maintenance valve and the floor of the containment box.
9) required more maintenance than any other component of the sampler-enricher.
The two-ply polyurethane boots which sealed the arm to the wall of 3A had to be replaced 17 times or an average of 3-1/2 times a year.
After standard maintenance procedures were established, the estimated cost per change was $1,800.[13]
During design the plastic boots were recognized as a potentially weak member that might rupture under the 40-psi design pressure conditions.
Radiation level inside 3A was $>100$ R/hr.
When the hand was assembled, the joint clearances were small (0.001 to 0.003 in.).
Before installation in the sampler, the leak rate from the buffer zone across both seals was $< 1$ cc/min at 40 psi pressure differential for each valve.
10) of the gates, increasing the leak rate.
The leak rate then decreased to less than 1 cc/min again, but gradually increased with use as dirt particles accumulated again.
Special ring-joint spring-clamp disconnects $^{14}$ developed at ORNL for remote maintenance usage were chosen for the valves.
Figure 11 shows the operational valve with the flanges and a pair of spring clamps.
A leaktight $(<10^{-4}$ cc He/sec) joint was obtained.
This required three clamps to be activated at one time to close the port and the other three to move 15 seconds later to seal its opposite side.
Figure 12 shows a 1C assembly.
When lC was pressure checked at 48 psig after installation, the load on each clamp was about 270 pounds.
For the configuration used, the clamps were rated for a load of about 300 pounds.
Prior to installation of the lC assembly, the access port leak rate was less than 1 cc/min at 40 psi differential.
Then a new cotter key was inserted through the 0.100-in.-diam hole in the pin and was bent to lock it in place.
However, the radiation level in 3A was so high (more than $100\mathrm{R/hr}$ at the manipulator port) that very little adjusting was done.

During one sampler-enricher maintenance period when the access port, the operational valve, and the maintenance valve were all open with the reactor at 1/2-psig pressure, a small quantity of radioactive gas was
The gas leaked past the 0-ring seal on one of the Knu-Vise cylinder rods.
The 4-in.-long plug had to be changed several times because of radiation-induced darkening.
The 1/2-in.-thick port at the periscope was only changed once.
Figure 14 shows the two pumps.
Special soft-seated solenoid valves were used which had low-leak rates through the seats even with a 40-psi pressure gradient on either side of the valve.
The 1/2-in. valves used in the off-gas system gave trouble mainly from foreign particles (metal chips) becoming embedded in the seat after installation.
Several coil failures occurred on the 1/4-in. valves.
Commercial-grade 3-way solenoid valves were used in the gas lines to the access port gas cylinders.
No person received an excessive dose of radiation from activities associated with the equipment even though radiation levels inside the equipment were high (<100 R/hr).
